title: Merging iNat CSV file with a Notion database
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"bglk0","text":"Allows merging an iNaturalist CSV file with a mycological observations database in Notion. The protocol was created for Mycosphaera's needs but can be easily adapted to other organizations.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: 1. Overview The main photo you choose for iNat will be the one that follows in Notion. The photo link will be available in the Excel file. You can then manually add other photos to the database if desired. The more complete your iNat observation is, the more the Notion database will be automatically filled and reduce your manual data entry workload. Basic principle: It is possible to match each iNat field with a field in the Notion DB, including data that iNat creates automatically (GPS coordinates, observation creation date and time, all data related to the phylogenetic position of the chosen species (from Kingdom to species). Tip: If you add your fungarium number in the tag field, you can follow it in the merging process. Advantages of this method: Fill the Notion DB with all observations from the day (or week or other chosen timeframe) in a single operation. Your iNat observations will be more complete if you get in the habit of writing short descriptions and ecological notes. iNat can take your observation without LTE network and synchronize later when you come back online The ability to integrate observations for a project all at once. Time savings. Disadvantages/Limitations: If the destination Notion column (property) is linked to another database, automatic entry may not work. This is the case, for example, with the species name which is linked, in Notion, to a database with all species names from Mycoquébec. You must therefore choose a temporary text column to import the data. For this specific case, we will export the Species Name data from INAT into the Suggestion column in Notion. Requires more computer manipulation (a bit more complex), but the time savings largely compensate when you have many observations to process at the same time.
Figure 1 - Inaturalist's download button In Inat, clic sur "Explore" then "Filters" and hit "Download". Figure 1 - Download button in Inaturalist
Select what observations will be added to the CSV file. Figure 2 - Options for observations selection to be added to CSV file.

Select the "fungi" checkbox and the one for "protozoa" (for myxomycetes), especially if you made other types of observations during your day (insects, animals, plants, lichens) that you don't want to import.

Enter your iNat username so that only your observations end up in the file. Note that it's possible to choose a project. It would therefore be efficient to create an iNat project for a specific inventory project. This way, it is possible to merge all observations for a particular project in one single operation!
Enter the desired date range. If it's a single day, enter the same date. Choose "range" rather than "creation date" since the creation date may differ in some cases from the observation date.

Choose only the desired data to speed up the process. Otherwise, for each unnecessary field, you'll need to indicate "Do not import" when linking fields in Notion. Depending on target database structure, wanted parameters may differ.
Géo: Figure 4 - Selection of geographic parameters
Basic: Figure 3 - Selection of basic parameters Note: id corresponds to the unique iNAT number. In the Notion database, it is automatically generated from the iNat link.
Taxon Depending on the Target database in Notion, chose taxonomic parameters to be eventually linked. Figure 5 - Selection of taxonomic parameters
Press the "Create export file" button and choose a suitable name and location on your computer. Figure 6 - Button to create the CSV file in Inat

Process by hiting "Map CSV headers" button. Figure 9 - Map CSV headers button in Notion The observations are now created in the Notion DB. Some fields will still need to be adjusted, but substantial time savings have just been made by creating observations in batches.

In Notion, using the search magnifying glass (or if you already have it in your favorites), open the "Mycological observations" database. Figure 7 - Opening the Notion Database Select the [ . . .] in top right then the "Merge with CSV" option. Figure 8 - Merge with CSV option in Notion Select the CSV file to merge from your hard drive.
creator: Jonathan Jensen-Lynch, MBA
